skill.name: hugging-face-jobs
skill.description: Guides users through running Python and container workloads on Hugging Face Jobs with hardware, authentication, persistence, and monitoring guidance.
skill.summary: Run cloud CPU, GPU, and scheduled workloads on Hugging Face Jobs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns. Review shows most high-volume alerts are Markdown documentation false positives, but the skill legitimately guides remote code execution on Hugging Face Jobs and token use. Publish with a medium-risk warning for remote execution, cost, network egress, and credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Remote Job Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to submit user-provided Python or container workloads to Hugging Face Jobs. This is legitimate functionality, but it can execute arbitrary user code on remote infrastructure and may incur cost.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly describe submitting inline scripts through hf_jobs and handling authentication. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so it is risky but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Credential Use in Remote Jobs
medium_findings.description: The skill documents passing HF_TOKEN as a secret and reading it from the job environment. This is needed for Hub operations, but users must avoid exposing tokens in scripts, logs, or environment output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file shows direct token access and secret passing patterns. The same section warns not to hardcode tokens, which lowers the risk from malicious to operational.

low_findings.title: Network Egress Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for posting job results to an external API and linking remote scripts. These are normal cloud job patterns, but they can move data outside Hugging Face if users choose unsafe endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples show outbound HTTP and remote script URLs, but they use placeholder or official-looking destinations. No evidence of hidden exfiltration endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger Static Command Alerts
low_findings.description: The large set of Ruby backtick, weak-crypto, path traversal, and dynamic import alerts come from Markdown code spans, documentation links, placeholder URLs, or example snippets. I found no executable package code or install hook in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review confirms the flagged patterns are in Markdown documentation and examples. The only file in the skill is SKILL.md, with no separate executable script present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inline Script Submission to Cloud Jobs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends passing script contents to hf_jobs. This can run arbitrary workload code when the user requests it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and central to the skill. It is dangerous only when used with untrusted or unreviewed scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Injection into Remote Environment
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends passing HF_TOKEN through job secrets so remote workloads can access Hugging Face Hub resources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly present and operationally sensitive. The guidance prefers secrets over plain environment variables, reducing severity.

content.value_statement: Running ML workloads often requires remote hardware, authentication, and persistence planning. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code prepare Hugging Face Jobs with safer defaults.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Hugging Face Jobs for Python, GPU, TPU, and container workloads.
Shows how to submit UV scripts and Docker-style jobs through the hf_jobs tool or CLI.
Guides HF_TOKEN secret handling for private repositories and Hub uploads.
Provides hardware flavor, timeout, scheduling, and cost planning guidance.
Covers result persistence to the Hugging Face Hub, object storage, or APIs.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for authentication, memory, timeout, and job monitoring issues.
limitations: Requires access to Hugging Face Jobs, which may require a paid plan.
Does not verify the safety of user-provided workload code before execution.
Cannot guarantee final job cost, runtime, or hardware availability.
Remote jobs need explicit persistence because job files are ephemeral.

use_cases.title: Run Batch Inference
use_cases.description: Prepare a Hugging Face Job that loads a dataset, runs model inference, and saves outputs to the Hub.

use_cases.title: Process Large Datasets
use_cases.description: Select hardware, dependencies, timeout, and persistence options for scalable data transformation jobs.

use_cases.title: Automate Recurring Workloads
use_cases.description: Create scheduled jobs for evaluation, reporting, synthetic data generation, or periodic repository tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Job
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run a simple Python workload on Hugging Face Jobs. Ask for any missing inputs before submitting it.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Batch Inference
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Hugging Face Jobs workflow for batch inference over my dataset. Include hardware, timeout, token, and persistence choices.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Failed Job
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Hugging Face Jobs failure and explain the likely cause. Suggest concrete fixes for authentication, memory, timeout, or dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Scheduled Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a scheduled Hugging Face Jobs plan for a recurring ML pipeline. Include secrets, monitoring, cost controls, and result storage.

output_examples.input: I need to run inference on a private dataset and save the results.
output_examples.output: A job plan with required token permissions, recommended hardware, timeout, persistence target, and a submission-ready workflow summary.

output_examples.input: My Hugging Face Job failed with an authentication error.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis that checks token availability, permission scope, repository visibility, and whether secrets were passed correctly.

output_examples.input: Schedule a daily dataset processing task.
output_examples.output: A scheduled job outline with cadence, hardware flavor, expected runtime, result storage, and monitoring steps.

best_practices: Use secrets for HF_TOKEN and avoid printing token values in job logs.
Start with smaller hardware and increase only after measuring memory and runtime needs.
Persist important outputs to the Hub, object storage, or an approved API endpoint.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode access tokens inside workload scripts or prompts.
Do not run unreviewed third-party scripts on expensive GPU hardware.
Do not rely on local job files after completion without explicit persistence.

faq.question: Does this skill run jobs by itself?
faq.answer: No. It guides an assistant to prepare or submit Hugging Face Jobs when the user requests remote execution.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it can launch remote code, use credentials, send network requests, and create costs.

faq.question: Does it require a Hugging Face token?
faq.answer: Only authenticated Hub operations need a token, such as private repository access or uploads to the Hub.

faq.question: Can it choose GPU hardware?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides guidance for selecting CPU, GPU, or TPU hardware based on workload requirements.

faq.question: Can jobs save their results?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends saving outputs to the Hugging Face Hub, object storage, or an approved API.

faq.question: Does it validate workload code for safety?
faq.answer: No. Users should review scripts, dependencies, endpoints, and token usage before running remote jobs.
